data.title: Taiwan Equity - Are Fundamentals Supporting?
data.description:
By Belle Change, Senior Manager, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

Taiwan equity outperformed APAC in 2023 and 2024 but sold off in 1Q25 and April. Valuation became more attractive as a result, but are fundamentals still supportive?

Outperformance vs regional peers in 2023 and 2024

As we discussed in our report last year (Divergence between Taiwan and Korea equity – the global growth bellwethers? (July 2024)), given the explosive AI-driven tech demand since 2023, Taiwan’s high concentration in semiconductors and tech hardware related stocks allowed its equity market to outperform the region in both 2023 and 2024. The Taiwanese equity market appears to have become more of a bellwether for global AI demand, which is relatively independent from the global economic cycle.

Exhibit 1: Major APAC And US Equity Total Return (USD)

The strong AI-driven demand was also reflected in Taiwan’s export data. As illustrated in Exhibit 2, Taiwan saw a strong double-digit growth in tech exports in 2020-2021, largely fueled by increased demand for work-from-home devices during the COVID-19 pandemic. However, Exhibit 1 shows that most equity markets posted negative returns in 2022 as many global central banks began their monetary tightening cycle. At the same time, the fading demand for remote work-related technology contributed to a slowdown in tech export momentum. In 2023, Taiwan’s export growth rebound sharply, boosted not only by rising demand for AI-related chips but also relevant supporting hardware.

Exhibit 2: Taiwan Export YOY (%, 3MMA)

In 2023 and 2024, FTSE Taiwan rose 32% and 33%, respectively, in USD terms (Exhibit 1). That said, being the strongest performer among APAC markets, Taiwan equity also became one of the most expensive markets in APAC in 2024, and expensive compared to its own history (Exhibit 3).

Exhibit 3: FTSE Asia Pacific 12m Forward P/E

Valuation became more fair…

Following a period of elevated valuations and a lack of new upside catalysts, Taiwan equities traded within a range during the second half of 2024. This was followed by a correction in February and March 2025. The US’s announcement of tariffs on China, Mexico and Canada on February 1st sparked a risk-off sentiment, while the release of DeepSeek’s R1 AI models raised market concern about the demand for non-Chinese AI supply chain. These have further fueled the correction of Taiwanese equities. The FTSE Taiwan Index declined by 13% from its January peak through April 2nd –“Liberation Day” when the US administration announced a new series of tariffs. The correction deepened after April 2nd, triggering a global equity sell-off and dragged Taiwan equities into a bear market. At its lowest point, FTSE Taiwan had fallen as much as 28% from its January peak before rebounding to its year-start level by late May.

This sharp correction has brought valuations back to more reasonable levels relative to regional peers. The 12m forward P/E ratio peaked at 19.2x in June 2024 – a level well above one standard deviation from the 10y average – and fell below its 10y average by March 2025 (Exhibit 4).

Exhibit 4: FTSE Taiwan 12m Forward P/E Ratio

As of May 2025, FTSE Taiwan, together with most other APAC equity markets, had recovered from the April sell-off, and was back to the level at the year start. The next question is whether the correction was solely due to the expensive valuation, or whether the fundamental outlook has in fact deteriorated meaningfully.

…while fundamentals remain supportive

As shown in Exhibit 5, the ROE of FTSE Taiwan increased to 16.1% in May 2025 (vs 12.6% in May 2024). Meanwhile, the valuation went back to a more reasonable level, allowing Taiwan equity to represent a more balanced risk-reward profile for investors compared to other APAC and developed markets.

Exhibit 5: P/E And Roe Of FTSE Taiwan Vs Major Equity Indices

If we break the index down into industries, it tells a similar story – most industries became more attractively valued. Over the past year, the valuation of the Tech industry, which accounted for more than 70% of FTSE Taiwan, became less expensive, while ROE rose from 16.5% to 19.7% as of May 2025.

Exhibit 6: Forward PE Vs ROE Of Major Taiwan Equity Industries Vs FTSE Korea

When examining another key fundamental indicator, it is true that the EPS growth forecasts has seen a gradual downward revision trend (Exhibit 7). However, a primary driver behind these revisions is the uncertainty surrounding US tariffs, a risk factor not only for Taiwan equities but also for broader regional markets, as highlighted in our quarterly report APAC Financial Markets Spotlight, May 2025 publication. Moreover, despite downward revisions, the EPS growth forecast for Tech industry remained at a level higher than what it was before the 2023 AI-driven upcycle.

Exhibit 7: Two-Year Forecast In EPS Growth Of Major FTSE Taiwan Industries

This resilience trend is also observed in the US Tech industry, where much of the global AI demand originates. Exhibit 8 shows that both EPS growth forecasts and net margins for US Tech remain strong. EPS growth forecast for the Technology Hardware and Equipment sector has been particularly high compared to history.

Exhibit 8: FTSE USA Index – Software And Computer Services And Technology Hardware And Equipment

This could suggest that AI-driven demand is structural and is expected to remain a key growth engine for the semiconductor space. This is important given Taiwan’s critical strategic position in the global tech supply chain. It is true that explosive growth is unlikely in the near term, as potential tariff-related headwinds could negatively impact overall chips demand and related capex spending. Nonetheless, in the longer term, as noted by TSMC, AI-related demand remains strong and continues to outpace supply, enforcing the view that the industry is positioned for sustained expansion over time. Exhibit 9 and 10 suggest that the capex of the five largest market cap Tech stocks in FTSE USA Index continued to see a strong trend.

Exhibit 9: Nvidia's Quarterly Capital Expenditure

Exhibit 10: Quarterly Capital Expenditure For Apple, Microsoft, Meta And Alphabet (USD MN)

Conclusion

Taiwan equity’s strong performance in 2023 and 2024 was underpinned by structural AI-driven demand, particularly in the Technology industry. While valuation concerns and external shocks — such as US tariff hikes — triggered a temporary sharp correction in 2025, the subsequent rebound and normalization in valuations have restored a more balanced risk-reward profile. Importantly, key fundamental indicators such as ROE and EPS growth forecasts, particularly within Tech, continue to reflect resilience and long-term growth potential. With AI demand proving to be structural and capex trends among global tech leaders remaining robust, Taiwan equities appear more attractively valued than they were a little over a year ago, despite near-term macro uncertainties.

data.title: Pfizer: An Offensive Play For A Bear Market
data.description:
I turned bullish on Pfizer, Inc. (PFE) in January 2024, shortly after CEO Albert Bourla went all-in on PFE stock by doing an intra-plan transfer in his Supplemental Savings Plan pension in December 2023 (for a total value of around $10 million). Since then, the company has made progress on its stated priorities, including adopting a more shareholder-friendly capital allocation framework, improving the operating margin by adjusting the cost base, integrating Seagen into the ecosystem before looking at other large-scale acquisitions, and maximizing the performance of recently launched new products. However, Pfizer stock has declined 15% since then as pessimism grew.

Today, amid rising geopolitical tensions and concerns regarding the sustainability of global economic growth, I find safety in Pfizer. In full disclosure, I am a growth investor, and I am not talking about safety in a traditional sense. I believe Pfizer offers a unique combination that is hard to match - defensive cash flows through a safe dividend, a rock-bottom valuation that has priced in all, if not most of the negative developments, and also exposure to positive surprises centered on its growth story.

Is Now The Time To De-Risk?

As an investor with an extensive investment time horizon, I have no reason to run away from stocks. Whenever there is a crash (think of the Covid crash), I have doubled down on my high-conviction picks. That said, tactical asset allocation is something I strongly believe in, which is why I try to build a portfolio that, in my opinion, is well-positioned to survive and thrive under different market conditions.

This brings us to the unique circumstances we face as investors today.

There is obviously the tariff threat. There is a lot of uncertainty about how the Trump administration's tariff playbook will impact the global economy but it is too early to rule out that tariffs will not have a meaningful negative impact on economic growth. Then we have the more imminent threat of geopolitical tensions. The situation in the Middle East seems chaotic as of this writing, and further escalations may force the U.S. to take a bold stance too. All of this suggests that investors should prepare their portfolios to weather a sharp market downturn in the worst-case scenario.

Recession odds are all over the place today. Based on treasury spreads, the implied probability of a recession in the next 12 months is below 30%.

Exhibit 1: Treasury-spread implied probability of a recession

Some institutional investors have already made strategic moves to prepare their portfolios for a downturn. For example, Ray Dalio placed a major bet on gold in Q1 by investing approximately $320 million in SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD). This comes after Bridgewater Associates fully divested its GLD holdings in late 2023. Stanley Druckenmiller's family office also reduced its equity exposure in Q1 by slashing the number of its holdings to 52 from 78. This is a major rotational move. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway, Inc. (BRK.B) (BRK.A) ended Q1 with a record cash pile of $347 billion as well.

While I also have exposure to gold, as I discuss in the next segments of this analysis, I now consider PFE as my preferred bet for a potential market downturn as it goes beyond just providing me safety in a downturn.

Pfizer Offers Defensive Cash Flows

The first pillar of my bullish thesis for Pfizer is the defensive cash flows it offers. The quarterly dividend of 43 cents per share, annualized to $1.72 per share, offers a yield of over 7% today. Pfizer has paid dividends in each of the past 35 years, which is as good a track record as it gets. With the 10-year treasury yield hovering around 4.4%, Pfizer's dividend yield compensates for the equity risk we are taking.

To evaluate the safety of the dividend, the first place to look is the management's commitment. Pfizer shifted its priorities to focus on shareholder returns last year. As such, Pfizer's first capital allocation priority today is maintaining and growing the dividend. As confirmed in the 2024 Annual Report, the capital allocation priorities are;

  1. Maintaining and growing the dividend
  2. Reinvesting in the business
  3. making share repurchases after deleveraging.

The decision to raise the quarterly dividend late last year also highlights the management's confidence in the current level of distributions.

Some investors, as I gathered by reading comments on some Seeking Alpha articles, are skeptical of the sustainability of Pfizer's dividend. To quantify the safety of Pfizer's dividend, I looked at the numbers from 2024 to calculate payout ratios.

Metric Value
Operating cash flow $12.74 billion
CapEx $2.91 billion
Free cash flow $9.83 billion
Total dividends paid $9.51 billion
Operating cash flow payout ratio 74.6%
Free cash flow payout ratio 96.7%

While the free cash flow payout ratio of ~97% may spook some investors, it is important to contextualize this ratio. Pfizer, arguably, is in a transitional business phase where the company is forced to weather the full impact of the massive decline in Covid revenue. On the other hand, Pfizer is aggressively investing in the Seagen integration and other R&D efforts to drive long-term revenue growth. The cost-cutting program is also gaining traction with leeway to improve. Given this context, I am comfortable with the free cash flow coverage of the dividend as I believe free cash flows will meaningfully expand in the next few years.

A Portfolio Of Non-Discretionary Blockbusters

The second pillar of my bull case for Pfizer is the strength of its portfolio. A careful analysis of Pfizer's portfolio reveals that this strength comes from products with deeply inelastic demand profiles. Some of these products include Ibrance for breast cancer, Xtandi for prostate cancer, Padcev for urothelial cancer, Vyndaqel for transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy, and Eliquis which is used as a stroke preventive medication.

This brings us to the patent cliff hanging over Pfizer. I discussed this in January 2024 as well, but we have more data to work with today, so let's have a close look.

Key products being challenged by patent expires are Eliquis, Ibrance, and Xtandi. For Eliquis, patent expiration will start in 2026, while for Ibrance and Xtandi, expirations are expected in 2027. While these products will carry Pfizer through a market downturn in the immediate future, beyond 2026, we are looking at a challenging environment.

Exhibit 2: Pfizer patent expirations

Given that Ibrance, Eliquis, and Xtandi alone accounted for more than 20% of Pfizer's revenue in 2024, addressing this gap is critical to ensure sustainable growth in the long run.

Pfizer, in my opinion, is building an impressive revenue bridge to add new blockbuster assets to its portfolio. What caught my eye is that Pfizer is not just trying to replace the expected loss in revenue but is also focused on replacing these assets with products that exhibit similar, if not even stronger, inelastic demand characteristics. This is exactly what you want to see from a potential safe-haven candidate.

Let's quantify the revenue at risk. From Eliquis, Ibrance, and Xtandi, Pfizer brought in approximately $14 billion in revenue in 2024. Going by standard industry practices, I estimate 80% of this revenue to be wiped off the table by 2030, so we are looking at a revenue gap of around $11.2 billion from these three key drugs.

Pfizer has already laid the groundwork to absorb this revenue hit. The oncology portfolio is quickly emerging as the cornerstone of Pfizer's response to patent expirations, with Padcev, which is already a blockbuster drug, expected to see a meaningful expansion in its addressable market opportunity with potential approvals for use in bladder cancer instances. While this alone could be a multi-billion revenue opportunity by 2030, I have decided to err on the side of caution to remain conservative as you will shortly see in my modeled revenue impact.

Elrexfio and Sasanlimab are also two potential blockbuster drug candidates within the oncology portfolio that I am keeping a close eye on.

Pfizer's enhanced vaccine portfolio represents another pillar of Pfizer's response. Abrysvo and the next-gen pneumococcal vaccine are two strong blockbuster candidates for the long run.

Pfizer is also eyeing high-potential new launches in other areas such as immunology, which include Velsipity, Litfulo, and Cibinqo that are aimed at chronic conditions such as ulcerative colitis, alopecia areata, and atopic dermatitis.

Using conservative estimates, here's how I project Pfizer's drug pipeline and recently approved products to bridge the revenue gap by 2030.

Drug/product Estimated 2030 revenue Comments
Vyndaqel/Vyndamax family $1.5 billion Modest growth while securing PTE.
Oncology $6 billion to $9 billion Assumes Padcev expansion and early contribution from Elrexfio and Adcetris.
Vaccine portfolio $3 billion to $5 billion Driven by Abrysvo and PCV vaccine adoption.
Immunology portfolio $2 billion to $4 billion

Based on these assumptions, I believe Pfizer is well-positioned to bring in total new revenue of between $12.5 billion to $19.5 billion by 2030, which, even in the worst case, would be sufficient to cover the expected revenue loss from the patent expiry for Ibrance, Xtandi, and Eliquis.

Market Weakness Will Open Doors For Offensive M&A

The third pillar in my bull case for Pfizer as a strong defensive play is centered on the company's strong balance sheet, which paves the way for strategic M&A deals should there be an opportunity to hunt bargains. As of Q1, Pfizer had $1.4 billion in cash and ~$16 billion in short-term investments. The company has already achieved its deleveraging target, so I can only assume that Pfizer will look for an opportunity to expand if a bargain lands in its lap. The sale of non-core assets, such as selling its stake in Haleon in Q1, should also boost Pfizer's excess liquidity.

Although Pfizer is currently focused on the Seagen integration and recently launched products, the management has explicitly stated that acquisitions can be on the cards. During the Q1 earnings call, CEO Bourla said:

Going forward, we are committed to building our cardiometabolic pipeline, including obesity by advancing internal programs such as our GIPR antagonist and pursuing external opportunities that could include partnerships or acquisitions.

Risks To Monitor

Risks facing Pfizer include a failure by the company to effectively and efficiently replace the lost revenue from patent expires, longer than expected Seagen integration challenges, and pricing pressures. The good thing is that I believe most of these risks are already priced into Pfizer's valuation, which leaves room for positive surprises. However, I will closely monitor the progress of Pfizer's pipeline as a lot depends on successful execution here.

Takeaway

Pfizer is ignored by the market today. Pfizer has what it takes to survive and thrive in the long term, and even more importantly, I believe Pfizer has all the characteristics of a strong contrarian play at a time when the risk of a market downturn is rising. The growth option embedded into Pfizer is what makes PFE a classic contrarian play. While certain risks need to be monitored closely, I am confident that a correction to the upside is a matter of when, not if.

data.title: Microsoft: Still Cheap After The Recent Rally
data.description:
Since my last coverage, Microsoft Corporation (NASDAQ:MSFT)(TSX:MSFT:CA) has been moving exactly as I expected: breaking all-time highs and heading towards my high-$400s price target before year-end.

In this article, I considered revising my bull case to understand if the stock is still trading at a reasonable valuation after the 22% jump since right before the release of FQ3 results.

I advance in this introduction that, even though I am disappointed by the recent tensions with OpenAI, I believe the market is looking beyond the dispute over Microsoft's equity in the $300 billion startup following the shift from its for-profit arm to a public benefit corporation.

While I agree that the recent friction has been a setback for the spotless narrative surrounding this stock, Microsoft has already started offering Elon's xAI model, Grok, to its cloud computing customers. Furthermore, the partnership with OpenAI extends through 2030, so I am not sweating a single drop about a near term deterioration in growth.

As CEO Satya Nadella recently said:

Every day that ChatGPT succeeds is a fantastic day for Microsoft.

Considering OpenAI's recent 9-figure deal with the Pentagon and the 20% revenue share agreement, I think Microsoft's management is more than happy with the partnership with OpenAI, as they have plenty of good reasons to stick with them. Also, now that xAI is in the mix, every win for Grok is a win for the company, too.

For the reasons I explain below, I reiterate my strong buy rating on Microsoft, although I warn readers that if Microsoft fails to make a deal with OpenAI before year end, my price target could be compromised.

Is Microsoft Still Cheap?

Since right before the release of Q3 earnings, shares are up by over 20%, and the RSI is signaling overbuying activity. Surely, the stock is overvalued, right?

Wrong.

On a P/OCF basis (I use operating cash flow and not my favourite, free cash flow metric, as hyperscalers are burning a significant amount of cash in their data center expansion strategy), Microsoft sits right in the middle of the Mag 7 group.

In fact, Microsoft is cheaper on this metric than Apple, and please consider that Apple is lagging behind the Magnificent 7 group since the comeback on April 9.

What I found even more surprising is the fact that the current valuation (P/OCF, TTM) is only slightly above the historical median for the past three years.

Let me put it another way: on a P/OCF basis, Microsoft is cheaper now than it was between Q3 2024 and Q1 2025, even though its share price has recently broken all-time highs.

When I look at the hyperscaler group, Microsoft is still half as cheap as Amazon on a P/FCF basis, although I admit that both Google and Meta look more attractive.

On the positive side, I believe the upcoming massive layoffs will improve the valuation of the company, although not this year, as severance payments will offset the immediate gains of cutting nearly 7,000 jobs worldwide.

A Cooling Relationship With OpenAI

I have little to no doubt that OpenAI would not be where it is today without Microsoft's backup in 2019 ($1 billion) and 2023 ($10 billion). According to Microsoft's latest 10-Q, the total funding commitments to OpenAI add up to $13 billion.

Recent reports are pointing to a rough patch in the partnership as OpenAI is structuring itself into a for-profit structure to make a future IPO possible. The tensions revolve around the equity Microsoft should receive in return for its $13 billion investment. Some sources are pointing to a range of 20% - 49%. Call me crazy, but that's a bit high considering that the start-up is valued at $300 billion.

The deal between OpenAI and Microsoft has a clear deadline: December 31, 2025. Why?

Well, OpenAI’s investor agreements, particularly the one with SoftBank, stipulate that it must convert from its existing capped-profit structure into a for-profit/public-benefit corporation (similar to Anthropic or xAI) by year-end. If they fail, "investors have the option to recoup some or all of the cash they have committed", according to this source. So, there is pressure in making a deal with Microsoft before year-end, and the recent rumors stating that "Microsoft [is] prepared to walk away from high-stakes OpenAI talks" are not particularly encouraging.

At the same time, Microsoft recently made available the Grok model to its cloud computing customers. What does this mean for the stock?

Not good news, in my view. While I am not overly concerned from a revenue perspective, as the partnership with OpenAI (including the 20% revenue sharing and licensing agreement to commercialize OpenAI's latest models through Azure), with or without a refreshed deal, will last until 2030, in the near term, these tensions could cool down the shareholder excitement following FQ3 earnings results. Just take a look at the chart below.

So far, the market is paying more attention to Microsoft's data center build out plans and their ability to secure power contracts for their data centers (which is their main bottleneck to burning through their CapEx, as I noted in my previous article). That's why shares have broken all-time highs without any signs of hesitation. That said, there is a big risk to consider.

How My Bull Case Could Go Bust

As I noted in the previous section, I believe the tension with OpenAI could lead to a deterioration in investor sentiment, especially if Microsoft and OpenAI don't make a deal before year-end.

This is a negative catalyst, and I recommend keeping a close eye on the progress of the negotiations and what is the market's reaction after the media release. A pullback to $470 (ex-resistance, now support) would make sense, but any selloff below this value would be a clear sign that momentum is drying up.

Conclusion

I reiterate my bull case on Microsoft, as I don't see clear signs of deterioration in the recent bull run following FQ3 results. Investor sentiment has not been affected (yet) by the friction with OpenAI, as the market weighs more the double-digit growth in Azure and the ability to spend the $80 billion in CapEx planned this year. The valuation is not stretched when you look at operating cash flow, and even on free cash flow, Microsoft is cheaper than Amazon.

Even though a pullback to $470 is likely if tensions with OpenAI escalate over the next weeks, I don't think the stock will lose its momentum. I believe funds are allocating capital to promising stocks, and right now, Microsoft looks like the top choice.

data.title: Rates Spark: Central Banks Are Just As Wise As The Rest
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder

Geopolitical risks pop up everywhere, but don't change the broader market view

The Bank of England kept rates on hold yesterday, with a voting split of six for a hold and three for a 25bp cut - a mildly dovish surprise. But as with the outcome for the Federal Reserve, the market impact from the decision was limited, with central banks just as wise as the rest of the market when it comes to the political and geopolitical uncertainties ahead. It will take an actual change in the data or significant news on trade or geopolitics to take us out of current trading ranges.

As for the situation in the Middle East, the headlines continue to dominate and oil prices have kept inching higher. Short-dated inflation swaps have continued to rise; the 2y EUR inflation swap has risen from 1.5% to over 1.8% in June. But this hasn't really dented the market expectation for one more cut from the European Central Bank.

The wider impact is visible, though relatively muted. Risk assets have been on the back foot again over the past sessions, and common “fear indicators” like the VIX are also somewhat higher. Bund swap spreads have only seen a modest further outperformance of the safe asset, as the 10y now yields 3.5bp below swaps. Implied rate volatility has already nudged back from its initial modest increase.

In eurozone government bonds, we have witnessed some rewidening, but some also on the back of headlines more related to domestic issues, like in France, for instance. But France aside, spreads generally remain at historically tight levels, with a 10y spread of Italian government bonds over Bunds still below 100bp - though coming from as low as 90bp earlier this month. The appetite for aggressive tightening positions at such levels will be limited going into a typically less liquid and more volatile summer period, especially considering the uncertainties around geopolitics and trade relations with key dates still coming up.

Dutch pension funds continued to increase interest rate hedges

The latest data shows that Dutch pension funds continued to add interest rate hedges in the first quarter of 2025. As funds are preparing to transition on January 1, 2026 or 2027, we expect them to stabilise their funding ratios through interest rate risk hedges. Upon transitioning, however, we expect a significant unwind of longer-dated hedges.

The two largest pensions, ABP and PFZW, with assets totalling around €800bn, still have relatively low interest coverage ratios. If these funds decide to follow the upward trend to protect their funding ratios, we can still expect strong demand for fixed receivers going forward. Given the new system sees less use for longer-dated (30Y+) hedges, the focus will likely be on relatively shorter tenors.

Friday’s events and market view

While geopolitical tensions simmer, the week will end on a quieter note in terms of scheduled events. The ECB will publish its economic bulletin and its data on money supply. More interesting could be the consumer confidence reading against the backdrop of international events. The US sees the release of the Philadelphia Fed Business index and the Conference Board’s Leading index. No primary market activity is scheduled.

The upcoming week could provide more insight as we will receive the flash PMIs for June, and on the geopolitical front, all eyes will be on the NATO summit in The Hague. European bond markets will also look to the updated issuance plans for the third quarter from Germany and the EU's funding plans for the second half of the year, and whether they will already reflect some of the prospective increased spending on infrastructure and defence.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Varonis: When Valuation Outpaces Growth
data.description:
Varonis Systems (NASDAQ:VRNS) is a well-positioned company in data security and unstructured information protection, with a solid technology platform, good progress on the SaaS transition and growing free cash flow generation. However, the current valuation already discounts a complete success scenario: expanding margins, competitive leadership and AI monetization. This makes the risk/return profile unattractive today.

Rating: Hold. High technological quality, but a price that already incorporates success.

Company overview

Varonis develops software solutions for the security and governance of unstructured data (documents, emails, multimedia files). Its cloud-native platform is designed to monitor, protect and control access to corporate data thanks to a proprietary metadata analytics engine and, more recently, integration with AI models (e.g. Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT API).

The company has successfully completed the transition from an on-premise to SaaS model: 80% of the expected Annual Recurring Revenue for 2025 will be cloud-based. The company is now focused on recurring SaaS revenue (61% of ARR as of Q1), with strong Net Revenue Retention from cloud customers and rapid adoption of high-value managed solutions like MDDR.

Financial Analysis

In recent years, Varonis has shown steady revenue growth, improving cash flows and a slow return to net profit. However, operating margins remain low. The company generated $56.4 million of free cash flow in Q1 2024 and $65.3 million in Q1 2025, showing solid progress, but EBITDA remains fragile and adjusted EPS remain at very low levels also in 2026.

This highlights a peculiar dynamic: cash generation is stronger than accounting profitability, a positive signal but also a potential distortion to interpret. In particular, investors are betting that the expansion of SaaS will lead to strong operating leverage from 2026 onwards, but this is not yet visible in the margins.

Key forecast data:

The chart below clearly shows how Free Cash Flow expectations (green line) have grown steadily, signaling analysts' confidence in the sustainability of the SaaS model and the scalability of the business. On the contrary, the EBITDA margin (orange line) remains very volatile and still at negative levels for a good part of the period analyzed, reflecting the operating costs related to the cloud transition and the maintenance of the commercial structure.

The estimated EPS (blue line), although improving, remains low: this confirms that full net profitability is still a gamble and that the bulk of the investment thesis is built on future margins and recurring growth, not on performance already demonstrated.

Varonis' earnings and cash flow estimates have improved in recent years, but profitability remains subdued relative to its market cap and growth. My view is that while I appreciate the financial discipline, the lack of real operating leverage over the next 12 months leaves the stock at a fragile valuation."

Q1 2025 Earnings Overview

Varonis reported strong Q1 results, highlighting continued ARR expansion and healthy free cash flow generation. According to the company's 10-Q filing, total revenue reached $136.4 million, up 19.6% year-over-year, driven by the ongoing SaaS transition.

  • SaaS revenue surged to $88.6 million, more than doubling YoY

  • Term license revenue declined to $31.5 million, consistent with the business model shift

  • Maintenance and services contributed $16.4 million

  • Total ARR hit $664.3 million, with SaaS making up 61%

The company also reported $65.3 million in free cash flow, and increased full-year FCF guidance to $120–$125 million.

However, from a profitability standpoint, GAAP operating loss was $43.8 million, compared to a loss of $47.6 million in Q1 2024. Despite improving topline performance and cost discipline, margins remain deeply negative, as the revenue shift to SaaS continues to weigh on short-term profitability.

In my view, cash flow strength is encouraging, and topline momentum is clear. But the GAAP operating loss remains substantial. Until Varonis can translate ARR growth into margin expansion, the investment thesis remains tied to a forward-looking rerating—rather than current earnings power.

Competitive and Execution Risks

The main risk is not a slowdown in growth, but a possible misstep. Unstructured data protection is a real need, but not an exclusive one: if generalist platforms like Microsoft Defender or AWS Security Hub were to bundle comparable tools with their ecosystems, Varonis’ differentiation would be eroded very quickly.

In an environment where:

  • IT budgets tend to favor all-in-one “platform” solutions,
  • macro risk dictates efficiency and consolidation of expenses,
  • AI adoption may be driven by larger and more integrated vendors,
  • Varonis may struggle to maintain pricing power, commercial traction and architectural relevance with enterprise customers.

In addition:

  • Net margins are still modest and the expected ROE of 6.9% in 2026 does not yet reflect a satisfactory return on capital;
  • Structural profitability remains a gamble, especially considering that competition is moving aggressively precisely in the AI ​​+ compliance area, where Varonis is historically strong.

I think that the real risk for the investor today is to buy a promising business, but with a valuation that leaves very little margin of safety if the trajectory is not perfectly linear.

Relative Valuation and Peer Comparison

To assess the justification for Varonis Systems’ current price, it is useful to compare its main market multiples with those of other key cybersecurity players.

EV/Sales: Only Apparently Balanced

Based on my own calculations using Bloomberg Terminal data, Varonis trades at approximately 7.07x its estimated 2026 revenue. Here's how the figure is derived:

  • Enterprise Value (EV): $5.11 billion

  • 2026E Revenue (Bloomberg consensus): $723 million

EV/sales = Enterprise Value/Revenue = USD 5.11 billion / USD 723 million = 7.07x

At first glance, this multiple may appear reasonable compared to top-tier peers like CrowdStrike (20.4x) or Zscaler (14.4x). But these comparisons can be misleading without considering fundamental differences.

Both CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Zscaler (ZS) offer:

  • Annual revenue growth above 20%

  • Double-digit EBITDA margins already achieved

  • Established technological leadership in endpoint and cloud network security, respectively

By contrast, Varonis is growing revenues at a slower pace (~16.5% CAGR through 2026), with EBITDA margins still below 10%, and remains a niche player in unstructured data protection. Until it demonstrates sustained operating leverage and competitive durability, its current EV/Sales ratio seems optimistic rather than conservative.

P/FCF and FCF Yield: quality is expensive

With a P/FCF of 50x and a FCF yield of 2.1%, Varonis is not in the bubble zone, but not at a discount either. CyberArk and Zscaler have similar multiples, but have a more robust growth history and less dependence on future execution (data from Bloomberg).

P/E: the most obvious issue

The expected Price/Earnings for 2026 is 135x, the highest of the entire group.

To put it in perspective:

if Varonis maintained an expected EPS of $0.37, and wanted to trade at a market P/E of 35x (more in line with PANW or S), the implied price would be:

0.37 x 35 = $12.95 per share

even assuming a more generous rerating to 50x P/E, the fair price would be:

0.37 x 50 = $18.50

This means that the current price ($49–50) already incorporates an EPS growth of about 300% further in the coming years, or a structural reassessment of the quality of the business that is not fully supported by the operating data at the moment (data from Bloomberg).

Revenue growth: not extraordinary

Varonis grows at 16.55% per year, a good performance, but lower than CRWD, ZS and SentinelOne (all above 20%). In a market that rewards scalability, this figure limits the possibility of further expansion of multiples (data from Bloomberg).

So...in my view:

Varonis is well positioned, but in a highly competitive market, having good fundamentals is not enough: operational excellence and defensible pricing power are needed. With multiples comparable to those of leading companies, but with lower margins and scale, the stock appears correctly priced.

The analysis by multiples suggests that the downside risk from disappointment is greater than the upside from perfect execution. And it is precisely this, in the current context, that justifies my Hold recommendation.

Risks to the Thesis

While Varonis has a strong product offering and a solid growth trajectory in ARR, there are several risks that could undermine the current investment case:

  • Execution risk on margin expansion: Despite the progress in SaaS transition and ARR growth, Varonis remains unprofitable on a GAAP basis, with a Q1 2025 operating loss of $43.8 million. If the company fails to deliver meaningful operating leverage in the coming quarters, current valuation levels could prove unsustainable.
  • Competition from large platform vendors: The unstructured data security niche is increasingly targeted by broader cybersecurity suites offered by Microsoft, AWS, and other large-scale vendors. These companies may bundle similar capabilities, reducing Varonis’ differentiation and pricing power.
  • Dependence on enterprise IT budgets: Varonis primarily serves large enterprises and public institutions. A slowdown in IT or cybersecurity spending, especially in a macro tightening environment, could delay new deals or renewals.
  • Valuation sensitivity: At over 130x forward earnings and 7x forward sales (Bloomberg data), the stock is highly sensitive to any deviation from growth or margin expectations. Even minor earnings disappointments could trigger outsized downside.
  • Long-term profitability still uncertain: Although free cash flow is improving, net income remains negative and ROE is not expected to exceed 7% by 2026. This raises questions on whether the business model can generate sustained returns on capital.

Conclusion

Varonis is undoubtedly an interesting company, with solid technology, a clear strategic vision and a growing customer base. Its AI integration and the mission-critical nature of its solutions give it a competitive advantage that is difficult to replicate in the short term.

However, the current valuation leaves little room for error: a bright future of growth, high margins and consolidated leadership is already “priced in”. In this context, the risk is not only operational, but also financial: the market has already assigned Varonis a premium that presupposes success. And when the price incorporates perfection, the risk of disappointment increases.

Rating: Hold. Varonis deserves attention and respect. But today it does not represent an attractive entry point for a value or contrarian investor. Better to wait for a more favorable entry point, or concrete signs of structural margin expansion.

data.title: VGSH: Short-Term Bonds For Real-World Survivability
data.description:
Intro

Short-term treasury bond funds, especially in the likes of Vanguard Short-Term Treasury Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:VGSH), offer a variety of de-risking benefits when included in a diversified portfolio. I just authored VGIT: Reducing The Risk Of Black Swan Events, which covers treasury bond funds in the intermediate-term length that address such benefits. However, there are some key differences between these two asset classes.

When asking the question 'what term length should I choose when selecting a treasury bond fund: short, intermediate, or long?', many refer to the adage of 'matching your investment horizon.' This indeed holds some truth, but I don't believe it tells the whole story. If that rule were followed strictly, then every investor who claims to have a long-term outlook would be heavily allocated to long-dated bonds. But we know behaviorally, that’s not how people operate. Investors live in the short term—even if they say otherwise. They care about this quarter, this year, and the next couple of years far more than a hypothetical thirty-year horizon.

Short-term Treasuries offer something that aligns with how people actually experience risk. They reduce portfolio noise. They prevent drawdown drag. And they offer a unique combination of liquidity and insulation that longer-duration bonds can’t quite replicate. Even Warren Buffett has commented on the value of short-term Treasuries in today’s climate, highlighting their utility as both a cash proxy and a protective sleeve during market stress.

Risk and Return Profile

Let’s start with a broader quantitative view. I modeled three portfolio types using factorlytics.io data spanning from August 1976 to June 2025: a 100% short-term Treasury portfolio (VGSH proxy), a 100% US Total Market equity portfolio, and a 50/50 hybrid portfolio between the two. This gives us insight not just into performance but also into behavior through cycles.

The VGSH proxy delivered a 5.36% CAGR with just 3.33% annualized volatility. Compare this with the US equity market, which produced an 11.5% CAGR but with 15.42% volatility and a brutal 50.89% drawdown. The 50/50 portfolio strikes a strong balance: a 9.17% CAGR, only 8.81% volatility, and a far more tolerable 24.11% max drawdown.

This balance is not just academic. It’s the reason portfolios stay intact. The higher Sharpe and Sortino ratios for the blended portfolio indicate that while investors gave up some returns, they were compensated with greater consistency, fewer emotional decision points, and better compoundability over time. This is where VGSH finds its role. It doesn’t seek to compete on return. It seeks to stabilize the journey.

Drawdown Management

Looking at how these portfolios behave during downturns tells an even more compelling story.

From the 1987 crash to the 2000 tech bubble to the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID drawdown, the equity-only line is characterized by deep valleys and long recovery paths. These are the moments where investors abandon their plans, sell at the worst possible time, and lock in permanent losses.

The VGSH proxy, by contrast, barely flinched during any of these events. Its drawdowns were mild, typically under 6%, and often recovered within a month or two. When paired into a diversified allocation, short-term Treasuries acted as an anchor, absorbing some of the shock and buying time for equity positions to rebound. It’s this ballast function that makes VGSH powerful not just in numbers but in practice.

Volatility and Behavioral Cohesion

Volatility is often spoken of in the financial media as if it’s just a number. But for real investors, it’s visceral. It’s the pit in your stomach when your portfolio falls 5% in a day or 20% in a month. It’s the doubt that creeps in at night after reading a recession headline. Short-term bonds like VGSH keep those moments manageable.

As the volatility chart shows, VGSH rarely exceeds 4% annualized volatility—even in extreme market environments. Equities, meanwhile, spike to 20%, 30%, and sometimes more. Even the blended 50/50 portfolio sees meaningful reductions in overall choppiness when VGSH is included.

That translates to less second-guessing and a greater ability to follow through on an investing plan. For those pursuing long-term compounding, behavioral cohesion matters just as much as allocation.

Tail Risk and Distribution Shape

Another layer of VGSH’s utility comes from its return distribution. Unlike equities, which have fat-tailed, left-skewed distributions that produce frequent extreme losses, VGSH has a tight, symmetrical distribution. Most of its monthly returns cluster around 0.5% with almost no tail exposure.

This translates to predictability. When markets crash and panic rules the day, investors holding VGSH experience relative calm. This kind of stability reduces the risk of panic selling, portfolio abandonment, or catastrophic missteps. It’s the kind of structure that doesn’t just protect capital—it protects decision-making.

Diversification Through Low Correlation

VGSH’s near-zero correlation with US equities is not trivial. The data shows a 0.09 correlation between short-term Treasuries and the US Total Market. That is close to statistical independence. When blended into a portfolio, this characteristic doesn’t just dilute volatility. It creates opportunities to rebalance, to sell high and buy low across asset classes.

The 50/50 portfolio shows this clearly. It captures a large portion of equity returns, but smooths out the ride considerably. The result is a structure that both grows and protects, something that becomes increasingly important in a fragile or overvalued market environment.

Peer Comparison and Structural Simplicity

VGSH is not alone in the short-term Treasury category, but it is arguably the most efficient. Compared to peers like Schwab’s SCHO or more tactical products like ProShares’ (TBT), (UBT), and (UST), (VGSH) maintains the cleanest profile.

It was launched in November 2009 by Vanguard and currently manages over $27 billion in assets. Its expense ratio of just 0.03% is among the lowest of any bond ETF in the world. In contrast, leveraged or tactical bond ETFs frequently carry expense ratios of 0.90% or more and often come with significant tracking error, leverage decay, and complexity unsuitable for long-term portfolios.

VGSH keeps it simple. It invests in a basket of 1–3 year U.S. Treasuries, carries no credit risk, and is entirely passive. There are no curve positioning bets, no derivatives overlays, and no speculative strategies. That transparency is a key reason why it remains one of the most widely held bond ETFs by advisors, institutions, and retail investors alike.

Takeaway

VGSH doesn’t get much attention during bull markets. It doesn’t generate excitement or make headlines. But it plays an essential role in long-term financial planning. It anchors a portfolio, dampens volatility, cushions drawdowns, and makes sticking to your plan more likely. And when the cycle inevitably turns, and volatility returns, VGSH is often the quiet asset keeping the ship steady.

In my own portfolio, VGSH plays an essential role. It serves as a stabilizer and a behavioral backstop. It gives me the option to rebalance into equities during fear-based selloffs and to maintain exposure to duration without the sensitivity of long-term bonds. In a market regime defined by higher rates, persistent uncertainty, and behavioral fragility, I believe VGSH deserves not just a mention, but a seat at the table.

In a tangible sense, I prefer to set aside cash in short-term treasuries that could potentially be used down the line. This could be things like emergencies, down payments on home or home renovations, or perhaps deploy more capital if I see market opportunities. Something like intermediate or long-term treasuries would be for retirement portfolios you won't touch for decades, in my opinion. But for the short term, I enjoy VGSH.

Attribution

I'm a data-focused investor and want to credit the following sources that supported the content and charts in this article:

FactorLytics

Vanguard

Seeking Alpha ETF Comparison Tools

data.title: PE Carve-Out Deal Value Rises As Companies Refocus On Core Operations
data.description:
Global private equity buyouts of corporate units, also known as carve-outs, have increased as corporations focus on balance sheets in an environment of shifting taxes, regulations and tariffs.

Private equity asset and business unit acquisitions amounted to $23.72 billion across 145 deals between Jan. 1 and June 3, higher than the roughly $19.37 billion across 127 carve-outs in the first five months of 2024, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

"A lot of corporates are reassessing their priorities and refocusing on core operations. And because of that, they're divesting noncore operations," said Bob Rivollier, partner at Ropes & Gray LLP, citing tax and regulatory changes and tariffs as drivers.

Rivollier expects more private equity carve-outs for the rest of the year, with a number of regulator-forced divestitures already underway that will create opportunities for private equity.

"We're seeing more forced divestitures from an antitrust perspective," Rivollier said. "There's a number of transactions and strategic combinations that are in the market right now that are going through [Federal Trade Commission] or [Department of Justice] review, and where as part of the government's approval of the transaction, they're requiring divestitures."

Global PE/VC-backed carve-outs, 2021–2025

Yearly

Quarterly

Data compiled June 4, 2025.

PE/VC = private equity or venture capital.

* Year to date through June 3, 2025.

Analysis includes global asset acquisitions, branch and business unit acquisitions announced between Jan. 1, 2021, and June 3, 2025., where the buyer/investor in the deal is or includes a private equity or venture capital firm.

Excludes terminated deals and add-on acquisitions.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

On the buying end, private equity is motivated to pursue deals due to ample committed yet unused capital. Private equity dry powder as of mid-June stood at $2.51 trillion, up from $2.42 trillion as of the end of 2024, according to Market Intelligence data.

"You have a whole private equity industry that is trying to do deals and deploy capital, and you combine that with corporates that are increasingly divesting noncore operations. You take those two together, you'll see an increasing volume," Rivollier said.

The US and Canada had the highest private equity carve-out deal value and volume in 2025, with 83 transactions totaling about $20.56 billion. Europe was a far second, with roughly $2.63 billion across 47 deals.

Industrials had the highest deal value, with two transactions totaling about $11.01 billion in 2025. Energy and utilities ranked the second-largest sector with about $2.92 billion across seven deals.

Largest private equity carve-outs

The largest private equity carve-out announced so far in 2025 is Thoma Bravo LP's proposed $10.55 billion acquisition of portions of the digital aviation solutions business of US airplane manufacturer The Boeing Co. (BA).

EQT AB (publ)'s (OTCPK:EQBBF) $4.25 billion deal to acquire the small cells solutions business of Houston-based cell tower operator Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) via the Swedish firm's EQT Active Core Infrastructure fund is the second-largest carve-out deal of the year so far.

The third-largest is Partners Group Holding AG's (OTCPK:PGPHF) $2.2 billion acquisition of a California portfolio of 11 natural gas power plants and operator Middle River Power from a fund managed by Avenue Capital Group LLC.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Procore Continues To Expand Their Product Features And AI Capabilities
data.description:
In my previous article on Procore Technologies (NYSE:PCOR), I highlighted the resilience of their business model and some of the future catalysts for revenue growth. In this article, I would retain my buy rating. I will discuss some recent developments after the Q1 2025 earnings release and share some of my observations.

Q1 performance review

In my opinion, PCOR's Q1 2025 earnings are solid and on track. A revenue of 311M (15%YOY) is within expectations. The adjusted operating margin of 10% and free cash flow margin of 47% also demonstrated their strength in terms of profitability. From the customer side, the gross retention rate of 95% continues to look good. Customers over 100k ARR, a key forward indicator, grew 14% to 2418 (2333 in the last quarter). International growth, an important element of the investment thesis, is progressing well with an 18% YOY increase. The management also mentioned their expansion into markets like Germany and the Netherlands, in addition to already strong markets in Australia and Japan. Overall, PCOR sustained its resilient business model, as its platforms are mission-critical, and its customers are well-diversified.

Discussions on AI and other product features

In the last call, the management talked about AI initiatives like Copilot and Agent Studio, with some theoretical use cases and a roadmap. In this call, they revealed some concrete steps they are taking. I am glad to see AI continues to be an important talking point in the call. It is important for the management to keep pushing this front and communicate their progress. The CEO is talking about real working AI agents that were developed to manage scheduling and ensure 'timelines are met'.

There is also expansion on their map features, which helps visualize geospatial insights for construction projects. The goal is to help with better documentation, planning, and coordination on specific job sites. This map feature is a native feature in their platform and not easy to develop from scratch. It could be attributed to their acquisition of Unearth Technologies in 2024, which indicates good developments on that synergy. Autodesk Construction Cloud (ACC) also has geospatial capabilities, but most of them are integrations (not specifically tailored for construction only).

Overall, Product features are the most important aspects for investors to track progress. PROC still has ACC as a legitimate competitor. They need to keep evolving their platform (like what they did this quarter) until ACC switches its strategies to not compete directly with PROC.

Growth levers are all intact

PROC has leading positions in a massive and fragmented construction market. It is a large fish operating in a large pond. The company should be able to find its growth runway regardless of the direction it chooses to take. The management estimates about 360k potential customers in its core market as of now. But they only have 17k onboard currently, still lots of green fields for them to fill in. This doesn't include large international expansion opportunities, in which PROC is investing heavily at the moment. Among current clients, half of the construction volume flows through PROC. This proves two things. First, the PROC platform is good; otherwise, no one would put 50% of their work on the platform. Second, there are still expansion opportunities to add a column, given the fact that the old/current project cannot be added to PROC. There is also much space for charging fees for additional features or higher pricing tiers. As PROC keeps adding new functionality and offering more value, PROC should be able to raise the prices of cross-selling those tools. All these are straightforward and proven growth levers that PROC can take.

Bottom Line

PROC's valuation has come down a lot. Historically, they are trading at higher EV-to-revenues multiples than Autodesk. Now, the stock price has dipped back to around the 60-70 range again, which makes PROC cheaper than Autodesk. While the go-to-market may undermine their cost structure and efficiency, it won't add significant weakness to the business. The buyback plan (100m per quarter rate) also continued with a 200m new amount authorized. I don't see any reason to downgrade my long-term buy rating.

In terms of risks, I think Trump's tariff policies will likely affect the construction industry. While PROC has not seen any material impacts yet, it could negatively affect their project pipelines. If tariffs (or trade wars) persist long enough, PROC customers may reduce and delay construction investment because of the macro uncertainties and changing consumer demands. CEO Tooey Courtemanche noted that they are confident in handling this, but I am not sure PROC is immune in any way. The CEO succession plan also adds near-term uncertainty. Personally, I think PROC needs a leader with construction experience, and I prefer that they pick a candidate from within. This is not a turnaround story; PROC is already a well-run company, and a new, inexperienced CEO may add more harm than good.

data.title: VOOV: The Current Value Of The Value Bias
data.description:
The Vanguard S&P 500 Value Index Fund ETF Shares (VOOV), launched on 09/07/2010 and managed by The Vanguard Group, Inc., aims to replicate the performance of the S&P 500 Value Index. It manages $5.5 billion and charges an expense ratio of 0.07%.

For broad equity market exposure, a fund with a value bias can be a good alternative defensive option these days. In the near term, equities face some challenges due to macroeconomic implications. VOOV offers exposure to more modestly valued stocks that might not be as volatile these days. However, it may benefit less from the medium-term tailwinds because of the lower tech sector concentration.

Portfolio

Because of the value-score methodology based on the P/E, P/B, and P/S metrics, VOOV excludes many of the companies you usually see in the top 10 holdings of an S&P 500 fund. Be that as it may, Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT) expose the fund to meaningful idiosyncratic risk as they are allocated 7.51% and 6.14% of it, respectively.

Predictably, a more modest tech allocation is observed with tech stocks representing 23.51% of the portfolio. Financials and Health Care have even more modest weights.

All in all, this is a better diversified portfolio than S&P 500 vanilla ETFs can offer. And its valuation is relatively attractive; it has a P/E ratio of 21.1x and a P/B ratio of 3.1x. The Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) has multiples of 26x and 4.7x, respectively.

Performance

If you look at the YTD performance of VOOV against that of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), you will see how it held up better as the trade war narrative weighed down on U.S. equities. But you can also see that SPY caught up to it when the market grew more optimistic lately.

The case for value is either based on very long-term passive investing or short-term tactical positioning to maintain a balanced exposure to the risks and opportunities. Of course, this article focuses on the second point. After the market recovered from the April low, it's now much closer to its all-time high and this positioning, coupled with current conditions, is a recipe for unattractive risk-adjusted returns.

Outlook

The severe drawdown noted above was driven by the across-the-board and reciprocal tariffs imposed by the Trump administration. Since then, the trade policy narrative has been very influential in market performance, although it was a significant driver earlier in the year as well.

And it's influential because it has important implications for economic and corporate earnings growth. In the first quarter of this year, real GDP experienced a QoQ decrease of 0.2%. However, Real Final Sales to Domestic Purchasers just decelerated by one percentage point from the fourth quarter of last year to 2%. The difference is explained by the second metric's exclusion of net exports.

The economic contraction indicated by GDP, therefore, captures the front-loading occurring in anticipation of higher tariffs. Aggregate demand has not been as bad within that context and the next GDP report is likely to confirm that by showing better-than-expected results as imports slow down due to the inventory build-up and the fact that businesses are generally unable to plan ahead right now.

You can see this is the case by looking at the declining orders for manufactured goods since early in the year.

Though the next GDP report may normalize the data we have available amid higher tariffs, slower growth is likely in the mid-term. The median forecast of professional forecasters for the second and third quarters is a GDP YoY growth of 1.5% and 0.9%, respectively.

Growth should eventually pick up again, but is not expected to reach the same level as in the recent past by the first half of next year.

A cooling labor market supports this case for slower growth than in the recent past. Payroll growth has been decelerating YTD and though unemployment is not too high at 4.2%, a shrinking labor force may be obscuring how high unemployment could really be once the participation rate stabilizes.

The labor market may be showing signs of cooling, but it's still tight and, ironically, this further supports the case for slower growth as it implies a potentially higher unemployment rate this year. This might seem contradictory, but you will have to take context into account.

The latest Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey [JOLTS] report shows that the non-farm job openings rate was at 4.4% in April, which was lower than it was at the start of the year. Employers are reluctant to hire, but they are also still reluctant to fire, since it has been so hard to find workers in the recent past. As the labor market continues to cool, letting go of employees under such economic uncertainty might get easier. Higher unemployment is also supported by the fact that the Department of Labor (DoL) has been reporting rising unemployment insurance claims.

Again, the labor market may be cooling, pointing to slower growth in the future, but it's still tight for its trajectory to be a good argument against the case for higher inflation in the near term. Unilateral and sectoral tariffs should result in a cost-push inflation increase. Yes, the CPI reading for May shows inflation rising only slightly to 2.4% and the Core CPI declining by 10 bps to 2.8% since April. But it's important not to let the recent data we have made us lose sight of the risk of higher prices.

It's normal for prices not to rise even after such a long time since the tariffs have been imposed. It will take some time before the pre-tariff inventory runs out. There is evidence that supports a two-month lag between tariff implementation and price increases. The next CPI report for June, scheduled to be released on July 15, should, therefore, reflect a more meaningful price growth acceleration.

Meanwhile, inflation expectations can also drive inflation up in the near term in a self-fulfilling fashion. The New York Fed Survey of Consumer Expectations points to a higher rate one and three years ahead.

Now, ~3% is meaningfully above the Fed's target of 2% and that explains its cautious stance right now. In the last meeting, the FOMC decided to hold the Fed Funds rate steady at a range of 4.25%-4.50%; that was the fourth meeting with no change in the interest rate policy. The central bank also updated the Summary of Economic Projections [SEP], in which the dot plot showed that the number of Committee participants expecting no cuts in 2025 jumped from 4 (March) to 7. Still, the dot plot indicates two cuts and an effective rate below 4% by the end of the year.

And despite the more hawkish message in the last SEP, interest rate markets assign a 58.8% probability for a cut in September, up from 51.5% 30 days ago. Some optimism is also reflected in the very narrow credit spread, despite the fear of a recession, as indicated by the flattish yield curve.

Sentiment & Positioning

The divergence between fund managers' and retail investors' positioning indicates that while professionals have quickly re-risked since April, retail investors remain more cautious and won't allocate back to pre-tariff levels.

Now, there's no evidence that the latter will get back to those levels as long as current conditions remain. This would require some sort of relevant catalyst and I haven't identified one that would move the needle.

As for sentiment, by looking at VOOV's recent performance, we can see that this is a cautious market. Though above its 50DMA and trending upwards since the April low, VOOV has been trending sideways lately.

However, the difference with other ETFs that track the S&P 500 is that its RSI shifts have been less erratic, suggesting bullish but modest momentum, and it has narrower Bollinger bands.

Putting It All Together

I doubt that VOOV will outperform its vanilla counterparts in the medium term, but it is reasonable to expect it to outperform on a risk-adjusted basis in the near term. Implications for slower growth and inflation going up urge for a cautious approach, which ETFs like VOOV can provide.

You should be aware that there is a risk of underperforming the market both in the short and long run if the trade tension narrative finds a resolution soon. A large part of the reason it recovered so fast since April has been the temporary trade agreement between the U.S. and China. A similar agreement with the EU can lift the pressure and improve sentiment, in which case VOOV could underperform the broader market.

Your Turn

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, then this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: U.S. Coal Markets Stage Modest Spring Rally
data.description:
US coal market prices rose slightly in May, despite natural gas prices easing month over month. Natural gas prices remain higher year over year and continue to support firmer steam coal demand.

Higher natural gas prices are expected to improve the economic competitiveness of coal generation through 2027, a welcome prospect for steam coal producers affected by low demand in recent years. Low natural gas storage levels this spring will add to the demand boost, but much of this will go to reducing surplus coal inventories.

Beyond 2027, the US coal market is forecast to face pressure from the expansion of zero-carbon electricity incentivized by the Inflation Reduction Act. Overall, the S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast projects 44.2 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, while 797.7 GW of renewable capacity is forecast to come online. Generation from coal plants is forecast to significantly decrease over this period, with US electricity demand for coal declining 46.8%, and coal plants accounting for just 7.1% of total generation by 2035.

The Take

Price trends: US coal prices experienced a modest increase in May, with export coal prices rising slightly while domestic coal prices saw minimal changes. Benchmark prices for coal in various regions, including Central Appalachian (CAPP) and Northern Appalachian (NAPP), indicated small increases, with export coal gaining $1.00 per short ton on average.

Natural gas market dynamics: Natural gas prices declined due to mild weather, impacting coal competitiveness. The Henry Hub spot gas price fluctuated but closed lower at $2.97/MMBtu. Regional discounts in gas prices persisted, affecting coal's market positioning.

Coal demand and supply outlook: The US Energy Information Administration (EIA) projected a reduction in coal stockpiles, indicating a potential clearing of surpluses by early summer. Despite a forecasted decline in long-term coal demand, there is an expected increase in coal shipments and production due to higher natural gas prices through 2027.

Regional production forecasts:

– Powder River Basin: Production is expected to grow modestly, reaching 215 million short tons (MMst) in 2025, with a significant decline in demand anticipated by 2030.– Illinois Basin: Stable production is forecasted through 2027, but demand is expected to decline sharply post-2027 due to wind generation expansion.– Appalachian Basins: Production is projected to remain stable at approximately 159 MMst, but a decline to 108 MMst is expected by 2030 as domestic demand decreases.

Long-term projections: The S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast anticipates significant coal plant retirements and an expansion of renewable energy capacity, leading to a projected decline in coal's share of total electricity generation to just 7.1% by 2035.

Coal prices saw modest changes in May, with export coal gaining slightly compared to natural gas. Benchmark export coal prices moved $1.00/short ton higher. CAPP region export benchmarks closed May at $79.00/short ton (up 1.3%), NYMEX CAPP at $77.00/short ton (up 1.3%), and NAPP Pittsburgh Seam 13,000 Btu per pound up $2.00/short ton (3.6%) to $57.00/short ton.

Domestic coal rose slightly, with Illinois Basin 11,500 mid-sulfur gaining $0.50/short ton, or 1.1%, to $45.75/short ton, while the NYMEX Powder River Basin benchmark held flat at $14.30/short ton.

Natural gas prices fell in May due to mild weather. Henry Hub spot gas opened at $3.12/MMBtu and moved to a mid-month high of $3.31/MMBtu before falling to close the month at $2.97/MMBtu. Spot prices averaged $3.13/MMBtu for the month. Storage refill picked up during the month, with working gas at 2,598 BCF as of May 30. This is 117 BCF above the five-year average, and 288 BCF below the surplus levels of 2024.

Regional gas market discounts continued in May. Chicago Gate averaged $2.88/MMBtu, a $0.25/MMBtu discount to Henry Hub. TCO Pool's discount eased to $0.43/MMBtu at $2.70/MMBtu, while TETCO M3's discount was $0.67/MMBtu at $2.46/MMBtu. SoCal Border's discount was $0.92/MMBtu for an average monthly spot price of $2.21/MMBtu.

The US EIA estimated February 2025 coal stockpiles at 107 MMst, a 7 MMst reduction from January. With another large drawdown expected for February, surpluses that built up during the past year could be cleared by early summer.

Current forward pricing for Powder River Basin coal reflects high inventories this year, with headroom to increase production against natural gas prices in 2026–2027. The inventory overhang may restrain price growth, but inventories may be normal by midyear. After 2027, declining demand will restrain price growth.

Bituminous coal price levels are primarily influenced by export markets, with today's price levels making domestic coal generation less competitive against Northeast natural gas. The recent rally in eastern natural gas prices may nevertheless boost first-half 2025 demand. Seaborne coal demand is expected to decline 6.7% relative to 2024 due to reduced demand from India and Mainland China.

Pricing benchmarks exceeding $65/short ton suggest sustainable returns for eastern bituminous coal. After declining last year, bituminous coal demand for electric generation is expected to remain stable through 2027 on higher electricity demand and more supportive natural gas prices. Declines in steam coal demand are expected to resume after 2027, and overall Eastern US coal demand is forecast to decline 62 MMst from 2025 to 2030.

Outlook for US coal production, demand

For the four weeks ending May 31, coal shipments averaged 10.4 MMst, 25% higher than this time last year. Continued strong deliveries indicate higher demand this spring and stronger coal shares due to higher natural gas prices.

The chart below compares the current production forecast with recent history. We forecast increased coal demand against higher natural gas prices through 2027. Gas to coal switching has already mostly cleared coal inventory surpluses, clearing the way for production growth this year.

We now forecast coal production at 524 MMst, an increase of 12 MMst (2.3%) from 2024 levels. Coal generation is forecast to further gain market share from natural gas through 2027, until relative coal and gas pricing normalizes and expanding green energy again puts pressure on coal generation. The overall coal market, including domestic demand and exports, is forecast to decline by 133 MMst between 2025 and 2030, which is a lower rate of decline than previously forecast.

Production outlook — Powder River Basin

First quarter 2025 production reports of the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) indicate quarter-end production at 54.9 MMst, an annualized rate of 219.4 MMst. Production is now forecast at 215 MMst in 2025, as demand more than offsets 2024 year-end inventory levels.

With inventory levels normalized, production is forecast for modest growth through 2027 against higher natural gas prices. By 2030, Commodity Insights projects that coal generation retirements in the Midwest and a substantial expansion of wind generation in Powder River Basin's core markets will shrink coal demand to 164 MMst, further declining to 143 MMst through 2035.

Production outlook — Illinois Basin

We forecast stable annual production through 2027, ranging between 74-76 MMst per year. After 2027, the expansion of wind generation incentivized by the Inflation Reduction Act and announced coal retirements are forecast to erode Illinois Basin coal demand. Coal production in the Illinois Basin is forecast to fall to 54 MMst by 2030, further declining to 33 MMst by 2035.

Production outlook — Appalachian basins

First quarter 2025 production reports of the MSHA indicate quarter-end production at 39.8 MMst, or an annualized rate of 159.2 MMst. With export into seaborne metallurgical markets forming an increasingly larger portion of Appalachian coal demand, the region's aggregate demand is less sensitive to natural gas price competition than in domestic producing regions of the Powder River and Illinois Basins.

While the Illinois Basin and Powder River Basin are forecast for improved demand against natural gas generation, gains in Appalachian coal will be more limited. We forecast production at 159 MMst, essentially the same as 2024. As remaining domestic demand erodes with only modest offsets from export growth, Appalachian production is forecast to fall to 108 MMst by 2030.

Further information

Market indicative coal forecasts by Commodity Insights represent forward curves for spot-traded instruments analogous to a strip of contracts. The shorter tenors - current year and prompt year, plus additional years, if available - are driven by the observed/assessed market.

The longer tenors - typically forecast years three to 20 for physically assessed markers - are driven by fundamental estimates of cash costs of production, accepted returns to capital, regional productive capacity, and forecast supply and demand. For the long-tenured portion of the curve, Commodity Insights forecasts prices for specific coal markers and defines the remaining markers via historical spreads.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
